text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Federal Funding Opportunities lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI work can often be positioned as a capability layer on top of data modernization, infrastructure, workforce, and equity investments.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI work can often be positioned as a capability layer on top of data modernization, infrastructure, workforce, and equity investments.
textPreview: AI work can often be positioned as a capability layer on top of data modernization, infrastructure, workforce, and equity investments.

text: AI projects often require resources that are easy to overlook: staff time, governance, training, validation, data modernization, monitoring, accessibility, community engagement, and sustainment. Funding strategy should make the full cost of.

text: Do not approve projects without confirmed pilot, scale, and sustainment resources.
textPreview: Do not approve projects without confirmed pilot, scale, and sustainment resources.

text:
Use this slide in the Federal Funding Opportunities training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 8, 9 and Tools 33, 32, 31.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Do not approve projects without confirmed pilot, scale, and sustainment resources.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Federal Funding Opportunities in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Federal Funding Opportunities training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 8, 9 and Tools 33, 32, 31.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI projects need funding for more than software licenses. Responsible implementation may require data modernization, cloud or secure infrastructure, staff time, training, governance, legal and procurement review, evaluation, monitoring, translation, accessibility, community engagement, and sustainment. Funding strategy should be developed before pilots are approved for scale.

Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

Position AI as an enabling capability within data modernization, workforce, equity, emergency preparedness, surveillance,. Match use cases to allowable costs in DMI, PHIG, HRSA, FEMA, EPA, SAMHSA, NIH, NSF, or state and local funding streams.

text: Technology licenses, hosting, security review, integration, and vendor support. Staff time for governance, implementation, training, monitoring, and evaluation. Community engagement, accessibility, translation, and communications support..

text: Who pays after the pilot? What staffing is required to keep the system safe and useful? What costs increase if the system scales across programs or jurisdictions? What functions can be shared regionally or through partnerships?

text:
Use this slide in the Federal Funding Opportunities training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 8, 9 and Tools 33, 32, 31.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Funding Alignment, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text: Use data modernization investments to strengthen interoperability, data quality, cloud readiness, and analytics infrastructure needed for AI.
textPreview: Use data modernization investments to strengthen interoperability, data quality, cloud readiness, and analytics infrastructure needed for...

text: Use infrastructure funding to support AI literacy, governance capacity, data staff, policy development, and implementation readiness.
textPreview: Use infrastructure funding to support AI literacy, governance capacity, data staff, policy development, and implementation readiness.

text: Design small pilots that map to allowable funding activities, measurable outputs, evaluation requirements, and sustainment plans.
textPreview: Design small pilots that map to allowable funding activities, measurable outputs, evaluation requirements, and sustainment plans.

text:
Use this slide in the Federal Funding Opportunities training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 8, 9 and Tools 33, 32, 31.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

What costs are required before pilot, during deployment, and after scale? Which funding streams align with data, workforce, equity, emergency response, surveillance, or service delivery goals? What happens when short-term funding ends?

text: Build a funding map for one approved use case that includes pilot costs, scale costs, sustainment costs, staffing, evaluation, governance, and possible funding sources.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Funding alignment table, Sustainment plan, Budget categories for responsible AI implementation.
Use this topic with Plays 8, 9 and Tools 33, 32, 31. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
